revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''アートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。 == 伝承 == ...'
revision.text:
'''アートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。
== 伝承 == 同じくアイヌ民話の巨鳥[[フリーカムイ]]が海上を飛んでいた時、あまりの騒音に怒って海面に出てフリーカムイと戦ったが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪の一覧]]
[[Category:妖怪|ああとしかむい]] [[Category:アイヌ|ああとしかむい]]

'''アートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。
== 伝承 == 同じくアイヌ民話の巨鳥[[フリーカムイ]]が海上を飛んでいた時、あまりの騒音に怒って海面に出てフリーカムイと戦ったが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪の一覧]]
[[Category:伝説の生物|ああとしかむい]] [[Category:アイヌ|ああとしかむい]]

revision.comment: ページ [[アートシカムイ]] を [[ラートシカムイ]] へ移動: 文献にて正しい名称を確認
revision.text:
'''アートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。
== 伝承 == 同じくアイヌ民話の巨鳥[[フリーカムイ]]が海上を飛んでいた時、あまりの騒音に怒って海面に出てフリーカムイと戦ったが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪の一覧]]
[[Category:伝説の生物|ああとしかむい]] [[Category:アイヌ|ああとしかむい]]

'''ラートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。
== 伝承 == 同じくアイヌ民話の巨鳥[[フリーカムイ]]が心が荒れすさんで空を海上を飛び回った際、海底で眠っていたラートシカムイがあまりの騒音に目を覚まし、怒って海面に出てフリーカムイと戦った。フリーカムイに墨を吐きかけたり、8本の足で海に引きずり込んだりしたが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという。
フリーカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、尾羽（アイヌ語でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪の一覧]] * [[フリーカムイ]]
== 参考文献 == * [[松谷みよ子]] 『松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話』 [[講談社]]、1995年、501-502頁。
[[Category:伝説の生物|らあとしかむい]] [[Category:アイヌ|らあとしかむい]]

[[画像:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|ラートシカムイの伝わる[[北海道]][[石狩湾]]]] '''ラートシカムイ''' は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。
== 伝承 == 同じくアイヌ民話の巨鳥[[フリカムイ]]が心が荒れすさんで空を海上を飛び回った際、海底で眠っていたラートシカムイがあまりの騒音に目を覚まし、怒って海面に出てフリカムイと戦った。フリカムイに墨を吐きかけたり、8本の足で海に引きずり込んだりしたが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという。
フリカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、尾羽（アイヌ語でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪一覧]]
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[松谷みよ子]]|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|year=1995|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1}} {{DEFAULTSORT:らあとしかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:北海道の妖怪]] [[Category:神話・伝説の水生生物]] [[Category:タコ]]

[[画像:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|ラートシカムイの伝わる[[北海道]][[石狩湾]]]] '''ラートシカムイ''' は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる。

revision.comment: 出典を参照して明確に
revision.text:
[[画像:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|ラートシカムイの伝わる[[北海道]][[石狩湾]]]] '''ラートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]{{Efn|アイヌ語でタコはアトイナウと呼ばれる<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>。}}。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。
== 伝承 == アイヌ民話において、アイヌの人々は巨鳥[[フリカムイ]]{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|松谷|1995}} では「フリーカムイ」と表記される。}}の棲み処に近づかず、穏やかな暮らしをしていた{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。しかしあるとき、ひとりの女性が道に迷ってフリカムイの水飲み場を汚し、怒らせてしまった{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。フリカムイが怒り狂って空や海上を飛び回った際、海底で眠っていたラートシカムイがあまりの騒音に目を覚まし{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}、怒って海面に出てフリカムイと戦った{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。フリカムイに墨を吐きかけたり、8本の[[腕 (頭足類)|足]]で海に引きずり込んだりしたが、力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。
フリカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、尾羽（[[アイヌ語]]でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book ja|author=松谷みよ子|authorlink=松谷みよ子|chapter=巨鳥退治|pages=500–506|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|date=1995-10-25|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1|ref={{SfnRef|松谷|1995}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=更科源蔵|authorlink=更科源蔵|title=アイヌの伝説|date=1971|publisher=[[北書房]]|asin=B000J94POY}}
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪一覧]] * [[アッコロカムイ]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:らあとしかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:北海道の妖怪]] [[Category:神話・伝説の水生生物]] [[Category:タコに関する文化]]

[[画像:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|ラートシカムイの伝わる[[北海道]][[石狩湾]]]] '''ラートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]である{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|アイヌ語でタコはアトイナウと呼ばれる<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>。}}。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。{{Harvtxt|更科源蔵|1971}}『アイヌ伝説集』に見える{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
ラートシカムイは「[[尾]]がたくさんある神」の意である{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。海で一番強い神であるとされ、陸で一番強い神であるフリカムイと争っていたとされる{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
== 伝承 == アイヌ民話において、アイヌの人々は巨鳥[[フリカムイ]]{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|松谷|1995}} では「フリーカムイ」、{{Harvtxt|朝里|2021}} では「フリー」と表記される。}}の棲み処に近づかず、穏やかな暮らしをしていた{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。しかしあるとき、ひとりの女性が道に迷ってフリカムイの水飲み場を汚し、怒らせてしまった{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。フリカムイが怒り狂って空や海上を飛び回った際、海底で眠っていたラートシカムイがあまりの騒音に目を覚まし{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}、怒って海面に出てフリカムイと戦った{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。フリカムイはラートシカムイの腕を嘴で掴んで陸に引きずり上げようとする一方、ラートシカムイはフリカムイに墨を吐きかけたり、8本の[[腕 (頭足類)|足]]で海に引きずり込んだりした{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。
フリカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、[[尾羽]]（[[アイヌ語]]でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book ja|author=朝里樹|authorlink=朝里樹|title=日本怪異妖怪事典 北海道|date=2021-06-25|publisher=[[笠間書院]]|isbn=978-4305709417|ref={{SfnRef|朝里|2021}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=松谷みよ子|authorlink=松谷みよ子|chapter=巨鳥退治|pages=500–506|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|date=1995-10-25|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1|ref={{SfnRef|松谷|1995}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=更科源蔵|authorlink=更科源蔵|title=アイヌ伝説集|date=1971|publisher=[[北書房]]|asin=B000J94POY }}
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪一覧]] * [[アッコロカムイ]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:らあとしかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:北海道の妖怪]] [[Category:神話・伝説の水生生物]] [[Category:タコに関する文化]]

revision.text:
[[画像:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|ラートシカムイの伝わる[[北海道]][[石狩湾]]]] '''ラートシカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]民話に伝わる巨大な[[タコ|蛸]]である{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|アイヌ語でタコはアトイナウと呼ばれる<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>。}}。[[日本海]]の海中に住んでいたとされる{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。{{Harvtxt|更科源蔵|1971}}『アイヌ伝説集』に見える{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
ラートシカムイは「[[尾]]がたくさんある神」の意である{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。海で一番強い神であるとされ、陸で一番強い神であるフリカムイと争っていたとされる{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
== 伝承 == アイヌ民話において、アイヌの人々は巨鳥[[フリカムイ]]{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|松谷|1995}} では「フリーカムイ」、{{Harvtxt|朝里|2021}} では「フリー」と表記される。}}の棲み処に近づかず、穏やかな暮らしをしていた{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。しかしあるとき、ひとりの女性が道に迷ってフリカムイの水飲み場を汚し、怒らせてしまった{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}。フリカムイが怒り狂って空や海上を飛び回った際、海底で眠っていたラートシカムイがあまりの騒音に目を覚まし{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=501}}、怒って海面に出てフリカムイと戦った{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。フリカムイはラートシカムイの腕を嘴で掴んで陸に引きずり上げようとする一方、ラートシカムイはフリカムイに墨を吐きかけたり、8本の[[腕 (頭足類)|足]]で海に引きずり込んだりした{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。力は両者とも互角で、決着がつくことはなかったという{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}。
フリカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、[[尾羽]]（[[アイヌ語]]でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book ja|author=朝里樹|authorlink=朝里樹|title=日本怪異妖怪事典 北海道|date=2021-06-25|publisher=[[笠間書院]]|isbn=978-4305709417|ref={{SfnRef|朝里|2021}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=松谷みよ子|authorlink=松谷みよ子|chapter=巨鳥退治|pages=500–506|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|date=1995-10-25|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1|ref={{SfnRef|松谷|1995}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=更科源蔵|authorlink=更科源蔵|title=アイヌ伝説集|date=1971|publisher=[[北書房]]|asin=B000J94POY }}
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の妖怪一覧]] * [[アッコロカムイ]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:らあとしかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:北海道の妖怪]] [[Category:神話・伝説の水生生物]] [[Category:タコに関する文化]]

フリカムイが海中に引き込まれた際、[[尾羽]]（[[アイヌ語]]でイシ）を左右に動かして（アイヌ語でカリ）ふんばったため、その付近の海を[[石狩]]（いしかり）と呼ぶようになったともいう{{Sfn|松谷|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|朝里|2021|p=72}}。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book ja|author=朝里樹|authorlink=朝里樹|title=日本怪異妖怪事典 北海道|date=2021-06-25|publisher=[[笠間書院]]|isbn=978-4305709417|ref={{SfnRef|朝里|2021}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=松谷みよ子|authorlink=松谷みよ子|chapter=巨鳥退治|pages=500–506|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|date=1995-10-25|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1|ref={{SfnRef|松谷|1995}} }} * {{Cite book ja|author=更科源蔵|authorlink=更科源蔵|title=アイヌ伝説集|date=1971|publisher=[[北書房]]|asin=B000J94POY }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ラートシカムイ]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11347744]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|The Ishikari Bay in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], where the legend of Ratoshikamui is told.]]
'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Notes ==

=== Annotations ===
{{Notelist}}
=== References ===
{{Reflist}}

== Sources ==
* {{Cite book ja|author=朝里樹|authorlink=朝里樹|title=日本怪異妖怪事典 北海道|date=2021-06-25|publisher=[[笠間書院]]|isbn=978-4305709417|ref={{SfnRef|Asari|2021}} }}
* {{Cite book ja|author=松谷みよ子|authorlink=松谷みよ子|chapter=巨鳥退治|pages=500–506|title=松谷みよ子の本 第9巻 伝説・神話|date=1995-10-25|publisher=[[講談社]]|isbn=978-4-06-251209-1|ref={{SfnRef|Matsutani|1995}} }}
* {{Cite book ja|author=更科源蔵|authorlink=更科源蔵|title=アイヌ伝説集|date=1971|publisher=[[北書房]]|asin=B000J94POY }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ラートシカムイ]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11347744]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|The Ishikari Bay in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], where the legend of Ratoshikamui is told.]]
'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

[[File:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|The Ishikari Bay in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], where the legend of Ratoshikamui is told.]]
'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11347744]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|The Ishikari Bay in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], where the legend of Ratoshikamui is told.]]
'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

[[File:ZenibakoKaisuiyokujo2004-11-29.jpg|right|thumb|220px|The Ishikari Bay in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], where the legend of Ratoshikamui is told.]]
'''Ratoshikamui''' (ラートシカムイ) is a giant {{ill|octopus|ja|蛸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octopus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タコ|1=Octopoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40152}} from {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is said to reside in the depths of the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]].{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Efn|In Ainu, octopus is called "Atoinau."<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=奥田統己|title=福島琴蔵（編）『アイヌ語の訳解』|journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|volume=236|pages=71–86|date=2022-10-31|url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/2000108}}</ref>}} It appears in {{Harvtxt|Sarashina Genzō|1971}}’s {{ill|Collection of Ainu Legends|ja|アイヌ伝説集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Collection of Ainu Legends|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

The name "Ratoshikamui" means "God with many tails."{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}} It is considered the strongest god in the sea and is said to have battled {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}, the strongest god on land.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight [[:en:Cephalopod_limb|arms]] to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}

== Legend ==
In Ainu folklore, the Ainu people lived peacefully, avoiding the habitat of the giant bird {{ill|Furikamui|ja|フリカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furikamui|qq=}}.{{Efn|{{Harvtxt|Matsutani|1995}} spells it as "Furikamui," while {{Harvtxt|Asari|2021}} spells it as "Furi."}} However, one day, a woman got lost and polluted Furikamui's watering place, enraging the bird.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} In its fury, Furikamui flew wildly over the sea and sky, waking Ratoshikamui from its slumber at the bottom of the sea.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=501}} Angered by the commotion, Ratoshikamui rose to the surface and fought Furikamui.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

The battle was fierce, with Furikamui trying to drag Ratoshikamui onto land by grabbing its arms with its beak, while Ratoshikamui sprayed ink and used its eight {{ill|Cephalopod_limb|en|Cephalopod_limb|WD=Q11630961|lt=arms}} to pull Furikamui into the sea.{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}} Their strengths were evenly matched, and neither could claim victory.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}

When Furikamui was pulled into the sea, it resisted by moving its tail feathers (Ainu: "ishi") from side to side ("kari"), which is said to have given the name {{ill|Ishikari|ja|石狩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikari|qq=}} to the nearby waters.{{Sfn|Matsutani|1995|p=502}}{{Sfn|Asari|2021|p=72}}
